sourcing.title: Unreasonable Tech Age Requirements
sourcing.description: The Hiring Manager demands a Senior Architect with 20 years of experience in Kubernetes. Kubernetes was released in 2014.
sourcing.speaker: Hiring Manager

choices.text: Start searching immediately. Time to find a time traveler.
choices.feedback: The manager is happy you 'can-do'. You, however, start looking for doctors who can manipulate physics.

choices.text: Gently explain that Kubernetes is only 12 years old.
choices.feedback: The manager sighs, 'I need someone who was thinking about it before it was released.' You negotiate it to 10 years.

choices.text: Suggest Docker/Containers flexibility instead.
choices.feedback: A compromise! You agree to look for general containerization skills. Sourcing speed improves.

choices.text: Cry quietly into your keyboard.
choices.feedback: You spend the afternoon sobbing. Surprisingly, it buys you time as the manager feels awkward and goes to lunch.

sourcing.title: The Prompt Architect
sourcing.description: A candidate claims to be a 'Senior Prompt Engineering Evangelist' and wants $350k, but their CV has no actual coding experience.
sourcing.speaker: Recruiter Colleague

choices.text: Pass them to the AI Team Lead for review.
choices.feedback: The Team Lead is insulted you sent someone who 'only knows how to chat with a website'. Your sanity takes a hit.

choices.text: Politely reject. We need actual software engineers.
choices.feedback: A clean reject. The candidate posts a 10-paragraph rant on LinkedIn about how the company hates AI innovators.

choices.text: Ask them to build a simple Python script to test them.
choices.feedback: They try to prompt ChatGPT to write the script, but submit it with 'Certainly, here is the script' still in the text.

choices.text: Hire them as a consultant to 'synergize' our team.
choices.feedback: The CEO loves the AI buzzwords, but the engineering team stops talking to you entirely.

sourcing.title: LinkedIn Thought Leader
sourcing.description: A candidate with a massive LinkedIn following and 'Open to Work' green banner asks for a fast-tracked interview loop.
sourcing.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Fast-track them to get some free social media publicity.
choices.feedback: They write a viral post: 'How I got an interview in 5 minutes'. Your inbox is now flooded with 4,000 resumes.

choices.text: Enforce the standard multi-stage process.
choices.feedback: They refuse to do the coding test and post: 'Why technical interviews are broken: A 10-part thread.' No one cares.

choices.text: Ask them to write a guest blog post instead of interviewing.
choices.feedback: They write it, but it's just plagiarized advice from a medium article. Sourcing time is saved, but CEO is unimpressed.

choices.text: Mute their profile and block their messages.
choices.feedback: A quiet day! You save your sanity, though they accuse our company of 'toxic gatekeeping' in a video.

sourcing.title: Crypto Bro AI Pivot
sourcing.description: A developer whose last five roles were 'Web3 Visionary' wants to join as an AI engineer, claiming 'tokens and weights are basically the same'.
sourcing.speaker: Hiring Manager

choices.text: Pass them to the engineering test stage.
choices.feedback: They try to solve the technical assessment using Solidity and ask when the company is launching its coin.

choices.text: Reject them immediately. Too much blockchain fluff.
choices.feedback: Manager is relieved. The candidate replies that we are 'missing the paradigm shift' and will go bankrupt.

choices.text: Suggest they apply for a product marketing role instead.
choices.feedback: They actually accept the interview for marketing! Sourcing time is saved, and the marketing lead is happy.

choices.text: Ask them about the details of their failed startup.
choices.feedback: They talk for 2 hours about a decentralized dog-walking app. You lose valuable sourcing hours.

sourcing.title: Yacht Remote Work
sourcing.description: Our star candidate says they will only sign if they can work 100% remotely from a boat in international waters.
sourcing.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Approve it immediately to close the sourcing stage.
choices.feedback: The legal department goes into a state of absolute panic over maritime labor laws and tax residency.

choices.text: Offer standard hybrid work and a free company hoodie.
choices.feedback: They decline. 'You cannot cage my spirit or my satellite connection,' they write back.

choices.text: Suggest contracting instead of full-time employment.
choices.feedback: They agree to work as a contractor. Finance is happy, and the candidate gets their ocean breeze.

choices.text: Ask if the boat has stable Starlink internet.
choices.feedback: They send you a 40-page PDF on network latency at sea. You wasted a lot of sourcing time.

sourcing.title: The Todo App Guru
sourcing.description: A fresh bootcamp grad applies with a resume listing 'Master of React, Node, and Python' but only has a Todo list project on GitHub.
sourcing.speaker: Engineering Lead

choices.text: Send them a junior-level take-home test.
choices.feedback: They copy the solution from GitHub. The tech lead is annoyed by the copy-paste code quality.

choices.text: Reject them. We need experienced engineers.
choices.feedback: The candidate is sad, but you save precious time for the engineering team.

choices.text: Offer them an unpaid internship first.
choices.feedback: They reject and call the company 'exploitative' in a local student forum. Your candidate rating drops.

choices.text: Set up a quick screening call to test their basics.
choices.feedback: During the call, they struggle to explain what an array is. Sourcing time wasted.

sourcing.title: Ex-FAANG Snobbery
sourcing.description: A candidate who spent 9 months at Google asks if we have 'free organic sushi' and 'nap pods' in our office.
sourcing.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Lie and say we are planning to build nap pods next quarter.
choices.feedback: They agree to interview, but they'll be very disappointed when they see the small kitchen.

choices.text: Tell them we have a coffee machine and a dream.
choices.feedback: They scoff and withdraw. The manager is glad you weeded out someone with an entitlement complex.

choices.text: Explain our hybrid flexibility instead.
choices.feedback: They are willing to compromise if we let them work remotely. You move them to the pipeline.

choices.text: Ask them if they can write code without Google search.
choices.feedback: A bit aggressive! The candidate feels insulted, and the manager thinks you lacked professionalism.

sourcing.title: The Infinite CV
sourcing.description: A resume lands on your desk containing every acronym since 1980, including Cobol, Kubernetes, React, and 'Quantum Blockchain AI'.
sourcing.speaker: Recruiter Colleague

choices.text: Ask them to explain their experience in detail.
choices.feedback: They reply with a massive, copy-pasted wall of text. Reading it ruins your day and your sanity.

choices.text: Filter out the noise and reject. Clearly keyword stuffed.
choices.feedback: Clean decision. You save the team's engineering hours from being wasted on a charlatan.

choices.text: Ask for a technical screening with a senior engineer.
choices.feedback: The senior engineer is annoyed because the candidate didn't know basic Git operations.

choices.text: Tell them to pick their top 3 skills and resubmit.
choices.feedback: They resubmit a clean, realistic resume. The process actually works.

sourcing.title: The Dota 2 Sourcing Strategy
sourcing.description: The Hiring Manager wants you to source candidates by looking at the top 100 players in competitive Dota 2, claiming 'high APM equals fast coding'.
sourcing.speaker: Hiring Manager

choices.text: Spend your afternoon messaging players on Steam.
choices.feedback: You get blocked by 90% of them. Sourcing sanity drops to zero, but the manager is happy you tried.

choices.text: Explain that game APM does not translate to system design.
choices.feedback: The manager complains that you lack 'out of the box thinking', but you save valuable sourcing hours.

choices.text: Compromise and look for candidates on GitHub with gaming side projects.
choices.feedback: You find a great candidate who built a game matchmaking bot. Sourcing is successful!

choices.text: Challenge the manager to a 1v1 game to decide the strategy.
choices.feedback: You lose the match in 10 minutes. You must now source from the Dota forums. Time wasted.

sourcing.title: The Boomerang Dev
sourcing.description: A developer who quit our company last month wants to return, but demands a 50% salary bump to do the exact same job.
sourcing.speaker: HR Director

choices.text: Approve the return. They already know the codebase.
choices.feedback: The manager is thrilled they don't have to train someone, but HR is upset about internal equity.

choices.text: Reject them. We cannot reward people for quitting.
choices.feedback: They go to a competitor. You now have to source a new candidate from scratch.

choices.text: Offer a 20% raise instead and some equity options.
choices.feedback: They accept! You balanced the budget and closed the role quickly.

choices.text: Ask them why they left in the first place.
choices.feedback: They spend an hour complaining about the office coffee. Sourcing progress slows down.

sourcing.title: The Certificate Collector
sourcing.description: A candidate's resume has 15 different Scrum Master, AWS Cloud Practitioner, and ITIL certifications, but no project history.
sourcing.speaker: Engineering Lead

choices.text: Set them up for a technical interview round.
choices.feedback: They fail the basic coding test. The interviewer is angry at you for wasting their afternoon.

choices.text: Reject them. Certifications are not a substitute for coding.
choices.feedback: A clean filter. You save time and focus on candidates who actually build applications.

choices.text: Recommend them for a Project Manager role instead.
choices.feedback: They are delighted! Sourcing is done and you helped fill a PM role simultaneously.

choices.text: Ask them to explain their favorite certification.
choices.feedback: They send you a 2,000-word essay about agile frameworks. Sourcing time wasted.

sourcing.title: Three Jobs, One Dev
sourcing.description: You suspect a candidate is overemployed because their LinkedIn profile has 'Currently Employed' at three different startups simultaneously.
sourcing.speaker: Recruiter Colleague

choices.text: Ignore it. If they can code, who cares?
choices.feedback: They join, but they are constantly absent from meetings. The manager is furious with you.

choices.text: Ask them directly about the overlapping timelines during sourcing.
choices.feedback: They get defensive and withdraw. You saved the company from a chaotic hire.

choices.text: Check references at all three current employers.
choices.feedback: You find out they are indeed collecting three paychecks. Candidate is blacklisted.

choices.text: Offer them a part-time contract instead.
choices.feedback: A great compromise. They agree to write code hourly, saving budget and sourcing time.

sourcing.title: The 15-Year-Old Genius
sourcing.description: You source an amazing candidate who built a custom database engine, but you discover they are only 15 years old.
sourcing.speaker: HR Director

choices.text: Hire them immediately as a full-time employee.
choices.feedback: The legal department goes nuclear over child labor violations. You are given a final warning.

choices.text: Reject them with a nice message wishing them luck.
choices.feedback: The manager is upset you let a 'genius' go. You restart the search for an adult.

choices.text: Offer them a summer high-school internship.
choices.feedback: A legal, happy solution. The candidate is excited and the manager gets help for cheap.

choices.text: Ask their parents to sign a freelance agreement.
choices.feedback: You get the code, but coordinating with their mother about tax returns is a headache.

sourcing.title: The Stealth CTO
sourcing.description: A candidate lists 'CTO at Stealth Startup' since 2023, but you suspect they are just unemployed and doing side projects.
sourcing.speaker: Hiring Manager

choices.text: Treat them like a senior dev and interview them.
choices.feedback: They do well in the coding test but demand a 'VP level' salary we can't afford.

choices.text: Politely decline. We need someone with corporate experience.
choices.feedback: You save time. Candidate sends a snarky reply about how we 'don't understand innovation'.

choices.text: Ask them to explain their startup product.
choices.feedback: They spend an hour explaining a blockchain-based shoe store. Sourcing time wasted.

choices.text: Offer them a standard mid-level developer interview loop.
choices.feedback: They swallow their pride and agree. You've sourced a solid engineer at a normal salary.

sourcing.title: The Green Banner Debate
sourcing.description: The Hiring Manager refuses to interview anyone with the green '#OpenToWork' banner on LinkedIn, calling it 'a sign of desperation'.
sourcing.speaker: Hiring Manager

choices.text: Comply. Filter out all green banners.
choices.feedback: You miss out on 70% of the active candidate pool. Sourcing speed crawls to a halt.

choices.text: Explain that the banner is a standard job-seeking tool.
choices.feedback: Manager rolls their eyes but lets you present a few banner-carrying candidates.

choices.text: Photoshopping the banner off their profile pictures before sharing.
choices.feedback: It works! They get hired. But your sanity is ruined from spending hours in MS Paint.

choices.text: Focus only on 'passive' candidates who are currently employed.
choices.feedback: Sourcing is slower, but manager is happy with the 'exclusive' feel of the pipeline.

sourcing.title: Hacker News Elitism
sourcing.description: The CEO wants you to post our job ad on Hacker News and hire whoever has the highest comment karma.
sourcing.speaker: CEO

choices.text: Post the job and start checking comment scores.
choices.feedback: You find a candidate with 50k karma. They spend the interview arguing about database index implementation. Sanity zero.

choices.text: Push back. Karma is not a metric for team collaboration.
choices.feedback: CEO is disappointed you aren't 'developer-native'. But you stick to standard sourcing.

choices.text: Post the job but use our standard application filter.
choices.feedback: You get great applicants and filter out the toxic trolls. Sourcing is successful!

choices.text: Tell the CEO he should handle the Hacker News thread.
choices.feedback: CEO gets into a flame war in the comments section and forgets about hiring for a week.

sourcing.title: The Theoretical Math Wizard
sourcing.description: A PhD in Category Theory applies for a frontend web developer role. They refuse to write CSS, calling it 'not mathematically rigorous'.
sourcing.speaker: Engineering Lead

choices.text: Hire them to write our database logic instead.
choices.feedback: They write beautiful, abstract database queries. The frontend lead is happy they don't have to talk to them.

choices.text: Reject them. A frontend developer must write frontend code.
choices.feedback: Clean reject. The candidate sends a math formula proving our recruitment pipeline is inefficient.

choices.text: Ask them to learn CSS over the weekend.
choices.feedback: They refuse. 'CSS is an insult to Turing completeness,' they write back. Sourcing time wasted.

choices.text: Suggest they apply to a research institution.
choices.feedback: You help them find a better fit. Sourcing time saved and candidate is grateful.

sourcing.title: The Sourcing Parser Failure
sourcing.description: Your ATS parsing software breaks on a candidate's three-column, emoji-heavy resume, converting their name into 'JavaScript Developer 🔥'.
sourcing.speaker: Recruiter Colleague

choices.text: Manually copy-paste their details into the system.
choices.feedback: It takes 40 minutes of your life. Sourcing time is wasted, but the candidate is in the pipeline.

choices.text: Reject the candidate because of bad CV formatting.
choices.feedback: You save time, but miss out on a potentially great dev who just wanted to look trendy.

choices.text: Ask the candidate to submit a plain text resume.
choices.feedback: They resubmit a simple Word document. The parser works and your sanity is saved.

choices.text: Just rename them 'Candidate A' and send it to the manager.
choices.feedback: Manager is confused why we are hiring a secret agent. Sourcing slows down.

sourcing.title: AI Outreach Fail
sourcing.description: Your automated sourcing tool sends an email saying: 'Hello {First_Name}! We loved your work at {Company_Name}!', and the candidate calls you out on LinkedIn.
sourcing.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Apologize publicly on their LinkedIn thread.
choices.feedback: Your public apology defuses the drama, but the company's brand image takes a small hit.

choices.text: Ignore the post and block the candidate.
choices.feedback: The post goes semi-viral. Other candidates mock your company. Recruiter sanity saved, but pipeline is damaged.

choices.text: Blame the intern in a humorous reply.
choices.feedback: The internet loves the joke. The drama dies down. Sourcing is back on track.

choices.text: Send a personalized apology email and offer a gift card.
choices.feedback: The candidate appreciates the gesture, deletes the post, and actually agrees to interview!

sourcing.title: The Failed Founder
sourcing.description: A candidate who was the CEO of a failed startup applies as a senior developer. Sourcing them might be tricky as they 'like to run things'.
sourcing.speaker: Engineering Lead

choices.text: Interview them for the dev role. They have great broad skills.
choices.feedback: They keep giving feedback on the company's business model during coding tests. Dev lead is annoyed.

choices.text: Reject them. We need a team player, not a boss.
choices.feedback: You avoid potential authority struggles in the team. Sourcing pipeline resets.

choices.text: Suggest them to the VP of Product for a Product Manager role.
choices.feedback: A perfect match! Sourcing is successfully completed, and the CEO is impressed.

choices.text: Ask them to present their startup's post-mortem.
choices.feedback: They spend two hours explaining why their VCs betrayed them. Time completely wasted.

sourcing.title: The Glassdoor Detective
sourcing.description: A candidate replies to your sourcing email: 'I saw your Glassdoor rating is 1.8. Why is employee turnover so high?'
sourcing.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Be honest. Tell them we had a bad manager who was fired.
choices.feedback: The candidate respects the honesty and agrees to talk. Manager is annoyed you blamed management.

choices.text: Say the reviews are fake and left by competitors.
choices.feedback: The candidate ghosted you immediately. Clearly didn't believe the corporate spin.

choices.text: Focus on the positive reviews and benefits.
choices.feedback: You sell the hybrid benefits. Candidate agrees to enter the pipeline. Sourcing closed.

choices.text: Delete your email and pretend you never saw it.
choices.feedback: You avoid the awkwardness. Sourcing time lost, but sanity intact.

sourcing.title: Weekend Warriors
sourcing.description: The Hiring Manager wants you to include 'Must love working weekends' in the job description to screen for 'true passion'.
sourcing.speaker: Hiring Manager

choices.text: Add it. The manager knows what they want.
choices.feedback: We get zero applicants for two weeks. Sourcing timeline is in critical danger.

choices.text: Refuse. That requirement will ruin candidate flow.
choices.feedback: Manager complains you don't support company growth, but applicant numbers remain healthy.

choices.text: Rephrase it to 'Occasional deployment support required'.
choices.feedback: Excellent corporate speak. Sourcing is successful and manager gets their weekend coverage.

choices.text: Suggest paying 1.5x salary for weekend hours.
choices.feedback: Finance rejects the budget expansion, but candidates love the idea. Sourcing delayed.

sourcing.title: The Crypto Salary Demand
sourcing.description: A candidate says they will only interview if we pay them entirely in Bitcoin or Ethereum, citing 'unstable fiat currency'.
sourcing.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Agree and write a custom contract.
choices.feedback: HR and accounting teams are crying over tax compliance and wallet security. Sourcing closed but chaos ensues.

choices.text: Explain that company policy only allows bank transfers.
choices.feedback: They decline and call you a 'fiat wage slave'. Sourcing pipeline resets.

choices.text: Offer a signing bonus in crypto instead.
choices.feedback: Accounting approves a one-time crypto purchase. Candidate enters the loop.

choices.text: Suggest they convert their salary themselves using a broker.
choices.feedback: They realize they can do that. Candidate is sourced, saving recruiter sanity.

sourcing.title: The Multi-Applicant
sourcing.description: A candidate uses an automation script to apply to all 47 open roles at our company, from Frontend Dev to Head of HR.
sourcing.speaker: Recruiter Colleague

choices.text: Review their CV to find the best match.
choices.feedback: You find they are actually qualified for a backend role, but your morning is gone. Sourcing time lost.

choices.text: Reject all 47 applications at once.
choices.feedback: A satisfying click. You save time, though the candidate leaves a bad review on Glassdoor.

choices.text: Ask them to choose one role and reapply.
choices.feedback: They pick the backend role. Sourcing proceeds normally.

choices.text: Block their IP from the application portal.
choices.feedback: You block them, but the IT department complains about recruiters managing network policies.

sourcing.title: The Scrum Master Overlord
sourcing.description: A certified Agile Scrum Coach applies. They say their goal is to replace coding hours with three additional daily syncs.
sourcing.speaker: Engineering Lead

choices.text: Fast-track them. We need process improvement.
choices.feedback: They introduce 'Fibonacci planning poker'. The developers stop writing code. Sanity drops.

choices.text: Reject them. Our engineers need to write code.
choices.feedback: The developers buy you a coffee to show their gratitude. Sourcing is successful!

choices.text: Set up an interview with the project team.
choices.feedback: The interview is just a 2-hour debate on Jira configurations. Time wasted.

choices.text: Ask them to explain their metrics of developer velocity.
choices.feedback: They fail to explain how meetings equal progress. They withdraw voluntarily.

candidate.title: The Coffee Shop Interview
candidate.description: A candidate joins the video interview from a crowded coffee shop. A barista is steaming milk and a dog is barking loudly.
candidate.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Push through the noise and continue the interview.
choices.feedback: You can only hear every third word. The interviewer's sanity is destroyed, but the candidate feels accommodated.

choices.text: Politely ask them to reschedule to a quieter time.
choices.feedback: They agree but are slightly embarrassed. Sourcing time increases, but sanity is preserved.

choices.text: Ask them to write their answers in the Zoom chat.
choices.feedback: The interview turns into a slow typing contest. Sourcing time lost.

choices.text: Mute their microphone when they are not speaking.
choices.feedback: Saves your ears! They get slightly annoyed at the constant muting, but the loop is completed.

candidate.title: AI Assisted Interviewing
candidate.description: During the live coding challenge, you hear the robotic voice of a screen reader and the candidate's eyes are moving suspiciously.
candidate.speaker: Engineering Lead

choices.text: Accuse them of cheating and end the call.
choices.feedback: They leave a furious Glassdoor review claiming discrimination. The manager is glad we caught them.

choices.text: Ask them to explain their code logic in detail.
choices.feedback: They fail to explain why they used a complex helper library. They withdraw silently.

choices.text: Change the question to a custom verbal logic puzzle.
choices.feedback: They fail completely. The team is happy with the filter, and candidate leaves quietly.

choices.text: Ignore it. Everyone uses AI at work anyway.
choices.feedback: They pass, but on day one they can't write a hello world. The manager is furious with you.

candidate.title: The Take-Home Rejection
candidate.description: Our star candidate refuses our take-home coding test, calling it 'unpaid labor' and asking us to review their public GitHub instead.
candidate.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Insist on the take-home. Rules are rules.
choices.feedback: The candidate drops out. Sourcing resets. Manager complains about losing talent.

choices.text: Approve the GitHub review instead.
choices.feedback: The engineering lead is annoyed they have to read undocumented code, but candidate is happy.

choices.text: Offer a $100 stipend for completing the test.
choices.feedback: Finance complains about the expense, but candidate completes the test and passes.

choices.text: Suggest a 1-hour live pairing session instead.
choices.feedback: Everyone agrees! Sourcing speed increases and the assessment is fair.

candidate.title: Interviewing with Mom
candidate.description: A junior developer candidate joins the Zoom interview. Sitting next to them is their mother, who says she is there 'to assist'.
candidate.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Allow the mother to stay and watch.
choices.feedback: The mother keeps interrupting to list her child's awards. Your sanity is destroyed.

choices.text: Politely ask the mother to leave the room.
choices.feedback: The candidate is very embarrassed and stays quiet the rest of the interview.

choices.text: End the interview immediately.
choices.feedback: You save time. Candidate's mom writes a letter to your CEO complaining about recruiter rudeness.

choices.text: Address all technical questions to the candidate only.
choices.feedback: You manage the meeting well. The candidate answers the questions, and the mom stays quiet.

candidate.title: Esoteric Programming Languages
candidate.description: During the coding test, the candidate asks: 'Can I write my solution in Haskell? I find Python too imperative.'
candidate.speaker: Engineering Lead

choices.text: Let them use Haskell.
choices.feedback: They write a brilliant 3-line solution. No one on our team can read it to grade it. Sanity drops.

choices.text: Insist they use Python or JavaScript.
choices.feedback: They struggle with the syntax but complete the task. The team can easily grade it.

choices.text: Ask them to explain their code line-by-line.
choices.feedback: They spend 45 minutes explaining category theory. You lose sourcing time.

choices.text: Suggest they rewrite it in pseudocode.
choices.feedback: A great compromise! Easy to grade, and candidate feels respected.

candidate.title: The Second Screen Eye Tracker
candidate.description: You notice the candidate is constantly looking to the right and reading text. You hear keyboard clicks but their hands are on their lap.
candidate.speaker: Recruiter Colleague

choices.text: Ask them to share their entire screen.
choices.feedback: They panic, make an excuse about system permissions, and exit the call. Loop closed.

choices.text: Ignore it and let them finish.
choices.feedback: They pass the interview, but fail the background check. Sourcing time is wasted.

choices.text: Ask them to type live on a shared notepad.
choices.feedback: They slow down significantly, but you confirm they are typing their own code. Recruiter gets clarity.

choices.text: Ask a question about their current screen layout.
choices.feedback: They blush and admit they had notes open. The interview proceeds honestly.

candidate.title: The Tables Have Turned
candidate.description: The candidate looks at the Team Lead and asks: 'What makes you think this company is worth my time?'
candidate.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Pitch the company's hyper-growth and culture.
choices.feedback: You talk for 15 minutes. Candidate nods sceptically. You saved the candidate's interest, but lost time.

choices.text: Turn the question back on them.
choices.feedback: The candidate is taken aback. The Team Lead is impressed by your negotiation skills.

choices.text: End the interview early. Too arrogant.
choices.feedback: A quick exit. Sourcing resets, but you protected your team's dignity.

choices.text: Offer to follow up with a detailed pitch document.
choices.feedback: You spend the night writing a pitch. Sourcing time lost, but candidate remains in loop.

candidate.title: Architecture Critic
candidate.description: The candidate spends the system design interview explaining why our current backend architecture is 'a pile of legacy garbage'.
candidate.speaker: Engineering Lead

choices.text: Defend the architecture and argue back.
choices.feedback: The interview turns into a shouting match about database indexing. Everyone is unhappy.

choices.text: Listen to their critiques and take notes.
choices.feedback: The candidate has great points! The engineering lead agrees they are a genius who can fix the system.

choices.text: Redirect them to the coding exercise.
choices.feedback: You keep the interview on track. The candidate completes the task and their critique is logged.

choices.text: Ask them to sketch their proposed rewrite.
choices.feedback: They draw a beautiful system layout. Sourcing timeline delayed, but candidate quality is high.

candidate.title: The Invisible Candidate
candidate.description: A candidate refuses to turn on their video camera during the interview, citing 'security concerns and bandwidth issues'.
candidate.speaker: Hiring Manager

choices.text: Insist they turn on the camera for identity verification.
choices.feedback: They refuse and withdraw. Sourcing resets. Manager is glad you avoided a potential scammer.

choices.text: Allow the voice-only interview.
choices.feedback: The manager is suspicious the entire time. They suspect the candidate is reading answers.

choices.text: Schedule a short live identity check before the loop.
choices.feedback: They turn on camera for 1 minute to show ID, then turn it off. Everyone is satisfied.

choices.text: Ask them to upload a video introduction instead.
choices.feedback: They refuse. 'I do not upload biometric data to corporate portals,' they reply.

candidate.title: The System Design Nap
candidate.description: During a long system design interview, the candidate goes quiet. You look at the camera and notice their eyes are closed and they are snoring.
candidate.speaker: Recruiter Colleague

choices.text: Scream 'Fire!' to wake them up.
choices.feedback: They jump up, spill their coffee, and disconnect. The interview is ruined.

choices.text: Cough loudly into your microphone.
choices.feedback: They wake up, apologize, and claim they had a medical reaction. The interview continues.

choices.text: Wait silently until the meeting time ends.
choices.feedback: You sit in silence for 20 minutes. Sourcing time wasted and manager is annoyed.

choices.text: End the call and email them to reschedule.
choices.feedback: They reply apologizing for their fatigue. Sourcing delayed, but candidate remains in loop.

candidate.title: Emotional Support Reptiles
candidate.description: The candidate wants to know if the office is pet-friendly, as they require their emotional support iguana to sit on their shoulder while coding.
candidate.speaker: Candidate

choices.text: Say yes, the office loves animals.
choices.feedback: The HR Director is furious about potential safety issues and terrarium space requirements.

choices.text: Say no. Only official service dogs are allowed.
choices.feedback: The candidate declines the role, stating the company culture is 'cold and non-inclusive'.

choices.text: Suggest they work 100% remotely with their pet.
choices.feedback: A perfect solution! The candidate is happy and the office stays reptile-free.

choices.text: Ask to see the iguana on the camera.
